feature.name: View Transitions: Inherit more animation properties

intake.finding: Genuine, attributable developer demand, not invented. Bramus filed CSSWG issue #11546 (2025-01-20) after hitting the problem in a real project: without animation-*: inherit on the ::view-transition-image-pair/old/new pseudos, the default cross-fade ran at a different timing curve than ::view-transition-group, desyncing animations, forcing a manual `animation-timing-function: inherit` snippet. The request was to extend the existing VT UA-stylesheet inheritance (which already covered animation-duration/fill-mode/delay) to animation-timing-function, -iteration-count, -direction and -play-state.
intake.limitations: Single high-signal author; no broader demand survey.

incubation.finding: Correct venue (CSSWG) and minimal design consistent with the existing VT UA-stylesheet inheritance pattern. Builds directly on the resolved precedent #9817 / feature 5424291457531904 (animation-delay inheritance, which shipped in Firefox), so the design space was already settled.
incubation.limitations: Exact CSSWG resolution text not retrieved (comment fetch failed); resolution existence is multiply-confirmed.

prototype.finding: Working implementations exist. WebKit landed the change (commit 034ea2b, reviewed by Anne van Kesteren) modifying Source/WebCore/css/viewTransitions.css to add the four `inherit` declarations; Chrome implemented it behind Finch flag ViewTransitionInheritAnimationProperties; WPT style-inheritance.html asserts all seven animation longhands inherit through the pseudo tree.
prototype.limitations: Chrome-side CL not directly inspected (tracking bug 427741151 was unreadable).

developerTrials.finding: No separate flag-based developer trial was conducted (no flag_name; finch_name only) or warranted for a UA-stylesheet interop change whose only observable effect is view-transition animation-timing synchronization. Validation came from the shared WPT test and cross-engine implementation parity, not a developer trial. This is a relevance judgment, not a claim that a trial was run and passed.
developerTrials.limitations: Relevance judgment; coverage routed through WPT/cross-engine parity rather than a dev trial.

wideReview.finding: A CSSWG resolution exists and is referenced by both the ChromeStatus entry and the WebKit implementation (comment #3005503138; the issue was on the CSSWG April 2025 agenda and is now closed, redirected to #12498). Safari: Shipped/Shipping (bug 296174). Firefox: No signal filed, but no concerns raised and the analogous animation-delay feature already shipped in Firefox.
wideReview.limitations: Exact resolution text not retrieved; no official Firefox position; tag_review_status recorded as 'Not applicable'.

experiment.finding: No origin trial (origintrial: false; all OT fields null). Appropriate for a UA-stylesheet interop fix, which does not require an origin trial.

prepareToShip.finding: Intent to Ship filed (stage_type 160, intent_stage 5; intent_thread_url present); Finch flag ViewTransitionInheritAnimationProperties set; rollout plan 'Will ship enabled for all users'; desktop/android/webview first milestone = 140.
prepareToShip.limitations: Intent thread body and LGTM count could not be read (groups.google.com URL rejected by the Z.AI reader); security_review_status and privacy_review_status were 'Pending' in the 2025-07-21 snapshot, though the feature subsequently shipped in 140, so those reviews presumably completed but are not confirmed.

release.finding: Shipped enabled by default in Chrome 140 (desktop, android, webview). Chrome 140 reached Early Stable 2025-08-27 and Stable 2025-09-02. The official Chrome blog documents the behavior precisely, including the updated UA stylesheet, and states the pseudo-elements 'inheriting more properties shipped in Chrome 140.'
release.limitations: No standalone announcement, sample, or doc link in the ChromeStatus record; release communication was folded into a broader October 2025 view-transitions summary post.

adoption.finding: A public Chrome usage counter exists but maps to the broader WebDX View Transitions family (ChromeStatus web_feature 'view-transitions' normalizes to webfeature property 'ViewTransitions', bucket_id 2; mappingStatus 'web-feature-family-counter', confidence 'declared-family'), not to this specific UA-stylesheet behavior change. No exact counter for 'inherit more animation properties' can fire by design, so adoption of the new behavior itself remains unmeasured; the family counter provides surrounding context for the View Transitions API family as a whole. Interpreted as the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads where the family counter fired, it stood at 0.00231316% at Chrome 140 stable (2025-09-02), dipped to 0.00165856% at day 30 (2025-10-02), then rose to 0.00523687% at day 90 (2025-12-01) and 0.00610281% at day 180 (2026-03-01); latest point 0.0052774% (2026-07-20), peak 0.00758282% (2026-04-04). The counter's first positive reading (2024-06-10) predates this feature, confirming it measures the wider family. No predeclared adoption target was stated for this feature, so the family level/trend is described rather than scored.
adoption.limitations: Family/property counter, not exact adoption of this change; the counter reflects the whole View Transitions API family. No exact-feature counter is possible by design. No unique-site, production-deployment, retention, task-success, user-benefit, or predeclared-target evidence exists for this specific change.

support.finding: No regressions, bug reports, or support issues specifically about this change were found within the evidence window. The Chromium tracking bug 427741151 could not be read to confirm post-ship status.
support.limitations: Absence of found issues is not evidence of a low support burden; the tracking bug was unreadable.

deprecation.finding: This is a behavior-additive CSS feature, not a deprecation or removal; no deprecation lifecycle applies.

developerValue.finding: Directly removes a real, attributable developer pain (desynced view-transition animations; manual animation-*: inherit boilerplate) with a minimal, ergonomic UA-stylesheet change: setting timing on ::view-transition-group now keeps the cross-fade of old/new pseudos in sync automatically.
developerValue.limitations: Small-scope value; no survey of how widely developers hit the problem.

endUserImpact.finding: Plausible small positive (more consistent, in-sync transition motion when developers customize group timing) with no accessibility/security/privacy harm surfaced: the VT pseudo tree is not exposed to the accessibility tree per spec, and this change only alters which animation longhands inherit. Impact is logical, not measured, and marginal for a UA-stylesheet change.
endUserImpact.limitations: No measured end-user evidence; benefit is plausible but not empirical.

adoption.finding: A ViewTransitions family counter exists but is not exact adoption of this UA-stylesheet behavior change (no distinct counter can fire by design). Interpreted as the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads where the family counter fired, it stood at 0.00231316% at Chrome 140 stable (2025-09-02), 0.00165856% at day 30 (2025-10-02), 0.00523687% at day 90 (2025-12-01), and 0.00610281% at day 180 (2026-03-01); latest 0.0052774% (2026-07-20), peak 0.00758282% (2026-04-04). With no predeclared target and no exact-feature, unique-site, production, or retention evidence, the adoption outcome for this specific change stays unscored; the measured family level is low but trended upward over the post-launch window. Low usage is not treated as failure and high usage is not treated as success; there is simply no threshold to compare against.
adoption.limitations: Family/property counter only; not equivalent to unique developers, sites, users, successful tasks, satisfaction, production deployment, or end-user benefit, and not exact adoption of this change. No predeclared target. 'Low but trended upward' describes the family counter, not a verdict on this behavior change.

interoperability.finding: Strongest dimension. The css-view-transitions-1 UA stylesheet now contains the exact rule inheriting all seven longhands; WPT style-inheritance.html covers them; WebKit implemented the change and its expected.txt flipped FAIL to PASS; Safari is Shipped/Shipping; Chrome shipped 140. Firefox has no official signal but raised no concerns and the analogous feature already shipped there, with same-document VT nearing Baseline.
interoperability.limitations: No official Firefox position; exact per-engine version matrices not confirmed.

implementationQuality.finding: Minimal, well-scoped change (a few UA-stylesheet lines), a reviewed WebKit implementation, extended WPT coverage, and no regressions found.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: Official release communication is accurate and precise (the Chrome blog documents the behavior, the milestone, and the UA snippet correctly). But the ChromeStatus record is sparse for a shipped feature: no motivation text, no docs/samples, security/privacy review 'Pending' in the snapshot, Firefox 'No signal', tag review 'Not applicable', and the Intent to Ship body could not be read to verify LGTMs.
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: Intent thread and tracking bug were unreadable via the Z.AI reader.

support.finding: No evidence of support burden either way in the window; no regressions or issues found and the tracking bug is unreadable.

overall.finding: A textbook small CSS interop fix that achieved its core goals - eliminating a real developer pain and converging on an interoperable behavior - with spec + WPT + two engines shipping near-simultaneously and no reported failures. Success is grounded in outcome evidence (interoperability, developer value, implementation quality), not shipment alone; adoption of the specific change has no exact counter (a ViewTransitions family counter exists as surrounding context but is not exact adoption), and support and end-user-impact remain unmeasured and are reported as such.
overall.limitations: Small-scope change; several process sources were unreadable; the only usage counter is a family counter, not exact adoption.

supports: Feature metadata, owner (vmpstr@google.com), category CSS, six lifecycle stages, intent_thread_url (stage 160/intent_stage 5), finch_name ViewTransitionInheritAnimationProperties, spec and WPT links, tracking bug 427741151, Safari Shipped/Shipping, Firefox No signal, desktop/android/webview first = 140, web_feature 'view-transitions'
sources.limitations: accurate_as_of 2025-07-21; is_released=false in snapshot (pre-ship); security/privacy review Pending in snapshot.

supports: Confirms summary, spec link, Safari Shipped/Shipping, Firefox No signal with rationale, tracking bug
sources.limitations: Same underlying data as the cached record.

sources.publisher: W3C CSS Working Group (csswg-drafts issue tracker)
supports: Original developer motivation and pain (Bramus, from a real project), proposed UA-stylesheet rule, references precedent #9817, on CSSWG April 2025 agenda, resolved/closed (redirected to #12498)
sources.limitations: Reader did not render comment #3005503138 (the resolution text); only the issue body was captured.

supports: Section 5 UA Stylesheet now lists animation-timing-function/iteration-count/direction/play-state as inherit on ::view-transition-image-pair/old/new; explanatory summary states timing set on ::view-transition-group dictates timing of all descendants
sources.limitations: Editor's Draft (living document); TR css-view-transitions-2 is the linked maturity URL but the UA rule lives in Level 1.

supports: Test asserts animation-duration/delay/timing-function/iteration-count/direction/play-state and fill-mode all inherit through the group/image-pair/old/new pseudo tree
sources.limitations: Shows test assertions, not per-engine pass/fail history.

supports: WebKit implementation in Source/WebCore/css/viewTransitions.css adding the four inherit declarations, reviewed by Anne van Kesteren, based on CSSWG resolution; WPT style-inheritance-expected.txt changed from FAIL to PASS
sources.limitations: WebKit only; lands in a Safari release.

supports: WebKit status RESOLVED FIXED; PR #48234; lists the same four properties; bug 294984 marked duplicate

Confirms pseudo-elements inheriting more animation properties shipped in Chrome 140; documents the updated UA stylesheet; broader VT context (React core <ViewTransition>, same-document VT nearing Baseline Newly available via Firefox 144)
sources.limitations: Post-hoc October 2025 summary, not a launch-day announcement.

supports: Existence of an Intent to Ship thread (from cached record stage 160 + URL)
sources.limitations: COULD NOT READ the body or LGTMs: the groups.google.com URL was rejected by the Z.AI reader ('Correct URL Format') and timed out; this is a known-unreachable source family.

supports: Existence of the feature tracking bug (from cached record)
sources.limitations: COULD NOT READ: repeated reader timeouts; status/details unknown.

supports: Directly analogous animation-delay inheritance feature cited as the template; per the cached record it shipped in Firefox, de-risking the follow-on
sources.limitations: Separate feature; not independently deep-read in this report.

sources.publisher: Chrome Platform Status webfeature popularity timeline (bucket_id 2 = webfeature property 'ViewTransitions', the WebDX View Transitions family counter; cached at evidence/metrics/timelines/webfeature-2.json, sha256 0d7ee6d0c10d1499e28574e0cf0f3cad5658a433f370cfdee0f5366295fd6100)
supports: A public Chrome usage counter exists for the broader View Transitions family (ChromeStatus web_feature 'view-transitions' normalizes to webfeature property 'ViewTransitions', bucket_id 2; mappingStatus 'web-feature-family-counter', confidence 'declared-family'). Interpreted as the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads where the counter fired, the daily percentage was 0.00231316% at Chrome 140 stable (2025-09-02), 0.00165856% at day 30 (2025-10-02), 0.00523687% at day 90 (2025-12-01), and 0.00610281% at day 180 (2026-03-01); latest 0.0052774% (2026-07-20); peak 0.00758282% (2026-04-04); first positive 0.000051% (2024-06-10). 578 timeline points.
sources.limitations: Family/property counter, not exact adoption of this specific UA-stylesheet behavior change (which cannot fire a distinct counter); the counter reflects the whole View Transitions API family and predates this feature. Values are the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads where the counter fired; not unique developers, sites, users, successful tasks, satisfaction, production deployment, or end-user benefit. Counter instrumentation may include detection/probing; UMA reflects participating Chrome clients and the dominant milestone, with enterprise/opt-out populations possibly underrepresented. Zero or delayed points are not proof of zero real-world use.

successes: Rooted in real, attributable developer demand (CSSWG #11546 from an actual project) rather than invented need.
Followed a proven template (precedent animation-delay feature 5424291457531904), reducing design risk.
Converged on a CSSWG resolution and spec edit, then validated with a shared WPT test that all engines run (WebKit FAIL to PASS).
Near-simultaneous Chrome 140 and WebKit shipping with matching UA-stylesheet rules produced genuine interoperability, not a Chrome-only behavior.
Accurate, precise official release communication in the 2025 view-transitions blog post.
failures: ChromeStatus record is thin for a shipped feature: no motivation text, no docs/samples, security/privacy review Pending in the snapshot, tag review 'Not applicable', no web-dev signals.
No standalone launch announcement; the feature was folded into a later (October 2025) summary post, so launch-time discoverability was low.
No official Firefox standards signal was filed (mitigated by the analogous feature already shipping in Firefox, but still a gap in the record).
counterfactuals: Had Chrome shipped the UA-stylesheet change unilaterally without the CSSWG resolution/spec edit, it would have created a UA-stylesheet interop gap requiring developers to keep the manual animation-*: inherit fallback for other engines; resolving in CSSWG first let Chrome and Safari ship the same behavior concurrently.
Without the shared WPT test covering all seven longhands, WebKit's initial FAIL on animation-timing-function might have shipped a partial implementation; the shared test caught it (FAIL to PASS).

If the team had treated 'Firefox: No signal' as a blocker, a low-risk interop fix would have been delayed despite a directly analogous feature already shipping in Firefox and no concerns being raised.

If no reviewer (Anne van Kesteren) had scrutinized the WebKit UA-stylesheet diff, the property grouping could have diverged from Chrome/spec; the small reviewed diff kept the implementations byte-equivalent in intent.
skillImprovements: Add a phase-collapsing heuristic for small CSS/UA-stylesheet interop fixes that follow an already-resolved template: auto-mark developer-trial and origin-trial phases not-relevant (with rationale) and stop them from dragging completion toward partial.
Add an optional precedentFeatureId field and a 'follow-on to shipped feature' fast path that reuses the prior feature's intent/spec/WPT scaffolding lineage.

Record known-unreachable source families (groups.google.com blink-dev intent threads fail the Z.AI reader's URL validation; bugs.chromium.org/issuetracker times out) and route them to the browser step instead of marking reports blocked; the skill currently permits the browser only for chromestatus.com - extend that allowance to blink-dev intent threads and the Chromium issue tracker.

Add a rule that down-weights a missing Firefox 'signal' when (a) a CSSWG resolution exists and (b) a directly analogous feature already shipped in Firefox, to avoid over-penalizing low-risk interop fixes.
Encourage a one-line motivation and a docs/sample link even on tiny features, so the ChromeStatus record is not the weak link in an otherwise clean launch.

Add a regression eval case based on this feature: a tiny UA-stylesheet interop fix should score success on interoperability/developerValue/implementationQuality, unscored on adoption/end-user-impact, and partial on completion due to unreadable process sources - not blocked.

Distinguish 'no exact-feature counter' from 'no public usage counter at all.' The original draft of this report asserted 'no use counter exists for a UA-stylesheet behavior change,' but a declared WebDX family counter (ViewTransitions, bucket_id 2) was always available. The skill should fetch any mapped family/property counter, record its exact scope (exact vs. WebDX family/property vs. origin-trial-only), and report the measured level/trend as unscored context with explicit caveats - never claim 'no adoption data exists' when only the exact counter is absent.

missingEvidence: Intent to Ship thread body and LGTM count (groups.google.com URL rejected by the Z.AI reader; not retrieved).

Exact CSSWG resolution text (comment #3005503138 / issue #12498; comment fetch returned no reader data, though the resolution's existence is multiply-confirmed by the ChromeStatus entry and the WebKit commit).
Chromium tracking bug 427741151 details/status (repeated reader timeouts).
Exact-feature adoption counter for this specific UA-stylesheet behavior change (none exists by design; the only public counter is the broader ViewTransitions family counter).
Unique-site, production-deployment, retention, task-success, and user-benefit evidence for this specific change (none found).
MDN documentation of this specific inheritance behavior (pseudo-element pages exist but the specific rule was not confirmed).
Post-ship regressions/support issues (none found, but absence of findings is not evidence of none).
